data.title: Petroecuador declares emergency at Ecuador's biggest refinery after fire damages
data.description:
Ecuador's state oil producer Petroecuador on Thursday declared a 60-day emergency at the South American nation's largest oil refinery, its second emergency in one month, after a fire this week caused damage to some fuel tanks.

Petroecuador said it had identified damages at two fuel tanks and in an electrical substation at its Esmeraldas refinery in northern Ecuador, which was affecting operational capacity.

"This led to the emergency shutdown of key units and auxiliary steam and power systems," the company said in a statement, adding the emergency period would allow Petroecuador to speed up its repair work.

Liquefied petroleum gas is being supplied according to availability and necessary scheduling would be assessed to attend to domestic demand, it said

Fuel prices are regulated by an external agency and have not changed, said Petroecuador.

Operations at the refinery have been suspended since Monday due to a fire. Petroecuador said at the time that all personnel had been safely evacuated and no injures were reported.

At the end of April, Petroecuador declared a separate 60-day emergency at the refinery due to damages from a magnitude 6.3 earthquake that struck the area.

data.title: Air New Zealand's Group Capacity Falls Over 2% in April
data.description:
Air New Zealand's group capacity fell 2.2% year on year in April, according to a Friday filing with the Australian and New Zealand bourses.

Long-haul available seat kilometers (ASKs) fell 2.3%, domestic ASKs dropped 3.8%, and short-haul international ASKs declined 1.1% year on year.

The country's flagship airline carried over 1.2 million passengers in April, down 4.3% on the year-earlier period, per the filing.

The passenger load factor was about 84%, an improvement from a little over 82% a year earlier.

The company's shares in the New Zealand bourse were down nearly 1% in recent Friday trade.

data.title: US probes effort to impersonate White House Chief of Staff, WSJ reports
data.description:
U.S. federal authorities are investigating an effort to impersonate White House Chief of Staff Susie Wiles, the Wall Street Journal reported on Thursday, citing people familiar with the matter.

The report said Wiles had told associates that some of her cellphone contacts had been hacked, allowing the impersonator to access private phone numbers.

The incident affected her personal phone, not her government phone, the report said.

The Journal reported that in recent weeks, senators, governors, top U.S. business executives and other figures received messages and calls from a person who claimed to be Wiles, citing the people familiar with the messages.

The White House and FBI did not immediately respond to requests for comment.

data.title: Japanese Shares Fall on Renewed Tariff Concerns
data.description:
The Nikkei 225 Index dropped 1.5% to below 38,000 while the broader Topix Index tumbled 0.9% to 2,785 on Friday, reversing gains from the previous session as renewed concerns over US trade policy rattled investor sentiment.

The selloff came after a US appeals court reinstated President Donald Trump’s global tariffs, overturning a previous federal court ruling that had found them unlawful.

Adding to the pressure, Tokyo’s core inflation rate exceeded expectations, reinforcing speculation that the Bank of Japan could proceed with additional interest rate hikes.

Markets widely anticipate a 25-basis-point increase at the BOJ’s July meeting.

Tech stocks led the losses, with Disco and Advantest both plunging 4.2%, and Tokyo Electron down 3.3%.

Broader declines were also seen among major names such as Fujikura (-2.5%), Sony Group (-2.4%), and Nintendo (-3%).

data.title: Motherson Sumi Wiring India Approves 1:2 Bonus Issue to Mark 50th Anniversary
data.description:
Motherson Sumi Wiring India said its board has approved a 1:2 bonus share issue as part of Motherson Group's 50th anniversary and in light of its strong FY2024-25 performance, according to a Thursday filing on the local bourses.

The bonus shares - one equity share for every two held - will be issued from free reserves, pending shareholder approval via postal ballot.

The company plans to issue 2.21 billion shares, raising its paid-up equity share capital to 6.63 billion rupees.

The record date and postal ballot details will be shared later. The bonus shares are expected to be credited by July 28.

** HMC stock up 0.8%

** HCW down 7.6% YTD

data.title: Ryman Healthcare Needs to Give Bolder Vision — Market Talk
data.description: If Ryman Healthcare has a vision for the next three to five years that goes beyond modest improvements in cash generation then management doesn't appear ready to share it with the market, says Forsyth Barr. It had hoped Ryman's FY 2025 result would offer clarity on the path ahead, stating it's "a raison d'etre to own Ryman through what is undoubtedly going to be a prolonged turnaround." Instead, Ryman's FY 2026 guidance disappointed. Ryman expects 1,200 total sales at the midpoint of its guidance range, substantially below Forsyth Barr's estimate of 1,350 ahead of its result. "The lower sales expectation is likely the core driver of Ryman's unwillingness to guide to positive free cash flow for FY 2026--another disappointment," analyst Aaron Ibbotson says. Forsyth Barr retains a neutral call on Ryman. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Mainfreight Likely Past Cyclical Earnings Trough — Market Talk
data.description: Mainfreight's earnings cycle appears to have troughed to Forsyth Barr. The logistics group's FY 2025 was mixed with a strong performance in Australia more than offset by profit declines in New Zealand, the Americas and Asia. Analyst Andy Bowley sees scope for Mainfreight's earnings to accelerate over the next 18 months, returning to trend growth in FY 2027. Still, Forsyth Barr warns this recovery is likely to be non-linear. "Uncertainty persists, particularly around tariffs, with a volume slowdown through May offset by strong forward bookings for June/July and accompanied by volatility in rates," Forsyth Barr says. It notes that Mainfreight is reluctant to talk about "green shoots" in New Zealand just yet. The bank retains a neutral call on Mainfreight. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Rongta Technology Launches Up to HK$221 Million Hong Kong IPO
data.description:
Rongta Technology (Xiamen) (HKG:9881) launched its initial public offering in Hong Kong Friday, seeking to raise up to HK$220.8 million from the deal.

The automatic identification and data capture company is offering up to 18.4 million shares expected to be priced between HK$10 and HK$12 per share, according to a Friday filing with the Hong Kong bourse.

Rongta expects to determine the IPO price on June 6 and disclose allocation results on June 9. Shares will begin trading on the stock exchange on June 10.

The technology company intends to use the proceeds to enhance research and development, boost production efficiency, widen its sales network, and fund working capital.

Rongta attracted Hongkong Linktech Technology, Hunan Dajing Power Technology Chief Executive Officer Cao Ke, Sinos Group, Main Achieve, and individual investors Lin Xiaojian, Li Shuhan, and Ng Chi Fat, as cornerstone investors.

Yue Xiu Securities, ABCI Capital, ABCI Securities, BOCI Asia, CCB International Capital, CMB International Capital, CMBC Securities, Huafu International Securities, Livermore Holdings, Patrons Securities, and Zhongtai International Securities are the joint bookrunners and lead managers of the IPO.

data.title: Westpac Faces Margin Headwind From Deposit Mix — Market Talk
data.description: A shift in deposit mix looks like being a stronger margin headwind for Westpac than for National Australia Bank, according to Morgan Stanley analysts. They point to Westpac's larger exposure to retail banking and the pricing of its retail savings accounts as justification for this view. The MS analysts observe in a note to clients that the proportion of savings in term deposits remains lower than at the end of the last decade, and that customers are still shifting toward higher-cost deposits such as those offering bonus enticements. This is one of the reasons that MS prefers NAB over Westpac among Australian bank stocks. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Trump calls Musk "terrific" and announces press conference with him Friday
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump praised Elon Musk on Thursday and said he will hold a press conference with the billionaire, whose tenure in the administration is ending, on Friday at 1:30 p.m. EST (1730 GMT) in the Oval Office.

"This will be his last day, but not really, because he will, always, be with us, helping all the way," Trump said in a post on Truth Social. "Elon is terrific!"

Musk announced he was stepping down from his White House stint on Wednesday, giving Tesla investors some succor after shares slumped this year in part due to the backlash to his support of Trump and right-wing parties in Europe.

The billionaire spearheaded Trump's so-called Department of Government Efficiency, charged with cutting federal spending.

On Tuesday, Musk criticized the price tag of Republicans' tax and budget legislation making its way through Congress.

In addition, he had recently pledged to spend less money on politics after he plunked down nearly $300 million on Trump's presidential campaign and on other Republican candidates last year.

data.title: nLIGHT CEO Scott H. Keeney Sells 75,000 Shares

Reporter NameKeeney Scott H
RelationshipPresident and CEO
TypeSell
Amount$1,149,750

Scott H. Keeney, President and CEO of nLIGHT, sold 75,000 shares of common stock on May 27, 2025, at a weighted average price of $15.33 per share, totaling $1,149,750. Following the transaction, Keeney directly owns 1,342,927 shares of nLIGHT. The sales were made under a Rule 10b5-1 trading plan adopted on June 12, 2024.

SEC Filing: NLIGHT, INC. [ LASR ] - Form 4 - May. 29, 2025

data.title: USD/JPY's Outlook Remains Bearish, Daily Chart Shows — Market Talk
data.description: USD/JPY's outlook remains bearish, based on its daily chart, says Fawad Razaqzada, market analyst at City Index and FOREX.com, in an email. This is indicated by downward sloping moving averages and bearish trendline on the chart, Razaqzada says. Also, USD/JPY's attempted breakout above this trendline has clearly failed, with the currency pair turning sharply lower, the analyst says. "USD/JPY could be on the verge of a more significant breakdown and this time it may even go on to take out that key 142.00-143.10 support area," Razaqzada says. USD/JPY is 0.2% lower at 143.85. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

2040 ET - USD/JPY's outlook remains bearish, based on its daily chart, says Fawad Razaqzada, market analyst at City Index and FOREX.com, in an email. This is indicated by downward sloping moving averages and bearish trendline on the chart, Razaqzada says. Also, USD/JPY's attempted breakout above this trendline has clearly failed, with the currency pair turning sharply lower, the analyst says. "USD/JPY could be on the verge of a more significant breakdown and this time it may even go on to take out that key 142.00-143.10 support area," Razaqzada says. USD/JPY is 0.2% lower at 143.85. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2023 ET - The yen strengthens against most other G-10 and Asian currencies in early trade, buoyed by signs of faster inflation. Data released earlier showed consumer inflation in the Tokyo metropolitan area picked up in May, signaling persistent price pressures nationwide. The data "suggests that the Bank of Japan may hike even earlier than our current forecast of October," Capital Economics' Marcel Thieliant says in commentary. "Our forecast is that this will happen in October, but the sheer strength of inflationary pressures leaves the door open for a July rate hike," the head of Asia-Pacific adds. USD/JPY falls 0.3% to 143.77; AUD/JPY declines 0.2% to 92.71. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2016 ET - Japanese stocks are lower as uncertainty over U.S. tariffs continues. Prospects for the Bank of Japan's continued rate increases are also weighing on the market following stronger-than-expected inflation data for Tokyo in May. Chip and tech stocks are leading declines. Renesas Electronics is down 4.7% and SoftBank Group is 4.1% lower. USD/JPY is at 143.83, down sharply from 145.68 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any developments related to tariffs and the BOJ's policymaking. The Nikkei Stock Average is down 1.6% at 37809.74. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

2006 ET - JGB futures rise in early Tokyo session, with Japan's government bond market likely to track overnight price gains in U.S. Treasurys. Both JGBs and Treasurys tend to move in tandem. The Finance Ministry's auction today of 2.6 trillion yen in two-year sovereign notes may garner interest from investors, analysts say. "Recent 2-year and T-bill auctions have cleared smoothly, or even drawn solid demand, and we expect a certain degree of collateral demand" at today's sale, SMBC Nikko Securities' Lisa Mochizuki says in a research report. Also, "factoring in the fact that there's limited scope for 2-year yield to rise further ahead, we expect a solid result," the junior analyst adds. Ten-year JGB futures are 0.31 yen higher at 139.26 yen. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1945 ET - Japanese stocks may fall as uncertainty over U.S. tariffs continues. Prospects of the Bank of Japan's continued tightening may also weigh on the market. Nikkei futures are down 1.4% at 37840 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 143.80, down sharply from 145.68 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any developments related to tariffs and the BOJ's policy making. Government data showed earlier that the consumer price index for Tokyo, excluding fresh food, rose 3.6% on year in May, compared with a 3.5% rise expected. The Nikkei Stock Average rose 1.9% at 38432.98 on Thursday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

1848 ET - Forsyth Barr worries Infratil's operating cash flow could worsen if it sells certain assets to realize some NZ$1 billion of proceeds. Infratil signaled the goal this week, stating that businesses unlikely to scale under its ownership are candidates for sale. Analyst Aaron Ibbotson thinks Diagnostic Imaging, Wellington International Airport, a 10% stake in Contact Energy, and RetireAustralia fit that description. "However, outside of RA, those three businesses provide the lion's share of operating cash flow," Forsyth Barr says. Infratil suggests its CDC data centers and Longroad Energy businesses could pick up the tab, but Forsyth Barr says that is many years away. "We applaud the clarified focus on having a sustainable operating-cash-flow position, but short of a dividend reduction we do not see a clear path," the bank says. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1653 ET - Mexico's benchmark IPC stock index edges down 0.2% to 58614 points, pulling back from yesterday's record-high close. Mexico and emerging markets in general have plenty of upside given low valuations, says Guilherme Valle, partner and portfolio manager at ABS Global Investments which has about half of its $8 billion assets under management in emerging markets. Mexico's pension system overhaul and measures to raise stock market liquidity are positives, while on the downside is a sluggish economy and the judicial reform, which "is a risk, but it's not going to be enough to derail everything that Mexico has going," he says. Mexican pension-fund allocation to equities is at an almost all-time low, Valle adds. "If you put growing pension size, a very low base in terms of equity allocation, very cheap valuation, and a pro-business or not anti-business type of government, that can be positive in terms of flows to the equity market." (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1628 ET - Two economists say the less-timely, but closely watched, Canadian nonfarm payrolls report for March is signaling a possible recession. Payrolls fell to a 10-month low after payrolls in March fell 54,100, or a third-straight month-over-month drop. National Bank Financial's Mathieu Arsenault says the data now indicate that over 70% of private-sector industries have shedding jobs. "Such broad-based diffusion of weakness has historically occurred only during recessions." David Rosenberg, head of Rosenberg Research, calls the payrolls data "a complete dud" that should compel the Bank of Canada to cut its interest rate next Wednesday, even though core inflation has accelerated above 3%. He adds the most domestically economic-sensitive sector, accommodation and food services, has faltered in the past 3 months. "There can be little doubt Canada has entered a recession," Rosenberg adds. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1625 ET - Goldman Sachs economists add a caveat to the rise in initial U.S. unemployment filings last week: "Jobless claims readings can be more volatile in the weeks surrounding the Memorial Day holiday," they write. The rise in claims to 240,000 last week roughly matches the highest seasonally adjusted levels recorded so far this year, but economists will want to see a bit more evidence from the volatile data series before overreacting. The May jobs report due Friday June 6 will provide a more complete look at the labor market. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1551 ET - Treasury yields fall ahead of inflation data and as GDP and jobless claims data indicate the economy is cooling, but not too fast, supporting the Fed's wait-and-see stance. Bid-to-cover ratio in a seven-year Treasury auction increases, signaling resilient demand. April annual PCE inflation, due tomorrow, is expected to slow slightly to 2.2% from 2.3%, in a WSJ survey. The core reading is forecast to remain at 2.6%. The University of Michigan consumer survey is expected to fall to 51.5 from 52.2. The 10-year yield falls 0.055 percentage point to 4.423% and the two-year sheds 0.052 p.p. to 3.938%, in both cases the lowest yield since May 9. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1528 ET - The policy backdrop grew more complex this week as a trade court blocked much of President Trump's tariff agenda--before an appeals court paused that ruling. Meanwhile, the White House says Trump told Fed Chair Jerome Powell on Thursday he wants lower interest rates. Neither headline will budge the Fed from its wait-and-see stance for now, Fed watcher Kevin Burgett of LHMeyer writes, projecting the Fed won't cut rates again this year. "We see no impact on our policy call from the meeting Trump had with Powell," Burgett writes. The trade-war litigation too will likely keep the Fed waiting for more clarity. "Even if this decision stands--which it may not--this isn't the end of Trump's trade war but rather a complication," Burgett writes. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1519 ET - In introductory remarks at a Fed conference in Washington Thursday, Federal Reserve governor Adriana Kugler says she remains attentive to a slow-burning story in the financial world: risks from commercial-real-estate mortgages extended to struggling properties. In many places, office buildings and other commercial venues haven't recovered from the changes Covid-19 wrought on the economy. Faltering debt threatens some banks and other lenders, Kugler says. "As we have seen in past crises, such as the Global Financial Crisis, vulnerabilities in specific sectors can have far-reaching consequences for the financial system," she cautions. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

data.title: Mainfreight Price Target Raised 8.5% to NZ$77.00/Share by Forsyth Barr
Waaree Energies Arm Bags $176 Million New Solar Module Order in US
data.description:
Waaree Solar Americas, a wholly-owned subsidiary of Waaree Energies , has secured an order worth $176 million from a US-based utility-scale solar and energy storage project developer and operator, according to a Thursday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

The contract is for the supply of 586 MW solar modules, which are scheduled to begin from the financial year 2026-2027.

data.title: Select Harvests Bull Buoyed by Planting Lull in California — Market Talk
data.description: Almond prices have gone nuts in recent months, and Bell Potter thinks a lull in planting in California can keep a lid on supply. The bank remains bullish on Australian grower Select Harvests even after a 43% share-price grain over the past 12 months. Analyst Jonathan Snape highlights that Californian plantings in 2024 fell to 1.2% of the bearing acreage, representing the lowest level since 1985 and materially below the 30-year average of 6.3%. It's now been below the replacement rate for three straight years. "This movement down in future supply growth potential has historically coincided with an inflexion in the commodity price and prolonged periods of upside in the commodity value," Bell Potter says. Select Harvests' 1H underlying profit was double the bank's expectations. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

International Gemmological Institute (India) Forms Subsidiary in Dubai
data.description:
International Gemmological Institute (India) has incorporated a step-down subsidiary in Dubai named IGI Diamonds and Gemstones Testing Laboratory LLC, according to a Thursday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

The new entity is a wholly owned subsidiary of International Gemological Institute FZCO.

The primary business of the new subsidiary is a diamond and precious gemstone testing laboratory.
